The *ken* is the distance between one supporting pillar and another, a quantity which can vary from shrine to shrine and even within the same building.
[History and Typology of Shrine Architecture](http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248), Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Shinto shrine architectural style

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Hiyoshi-taisha_higashihongu-honden01nt3200.jpg/330px-Hiyoshi-taisha_higashihongu-honden01nt3200.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hiyoshi-taisha_higashihongu-honden01nt3200.jpg)

Hiyoshi Taisha's Higashi Hon-gū

***Hiyoshi-zukuri*** or ***hie-zukuri*** (日吉造), also called *shōtei-zukuri* / *shōtai-zukuri* (聖帝造) or *sannō-zukuri* (山王造) is a rare [Shinto shrine](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine") architectural style presently found in only three instances, all at [Hiyoshi Taisha](/wiki/Hiyoshi_Taisha "Hiyoshi Taisha") in [Ōtsu, Shiga](/wiki/%C5%8Ctsu,_Shiga "Ōtsu, Shiga"), hence the name. They are the East and West Honden Hon-gū (本殿本宮) and the Sessha Usa Jingū Honden (摂社宇佐神宮本殿).

It is characterized by a [hip-and-gable roof](/wiki/Gablet_roof "Gablet roof") with verandas called *[hisashi](/wiki/Hisashi_(architecture) "Hisashi (architecture)")* on the sides.[[1]](#cite_note-kojien-1) It has a *[hirairi](/wiki/Hirairi "Hirairi")* structure, that is, the building has its main entrance on the side which runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side).

The building is composed of a 3x2 *[ken](/wiki/Ken_(architecture) "Ken (architecture)")*[[2]](#cite_note-2) core called *[moya](/wiki/Moya_(architecture) "Moya (architecture)")* surrounded on three sides by a 1-*ken* wide *hisashi*, totaling 5x3 *ken* (see photo).[[3]](#cite_note-jahi-3) The three-sided *hisashi* is unique and typical of this style. The gabled roof extends in small [porticos](/wiki/Portico "Portico") on the front and the two gabled sides.[[1]](#cite_note-kojien-1)[[4]](#cite_note-ko-4) The roof on the back has a characteristic shape (see photo below).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Hiyoshi-taisha_nishihongu2.jpg/250px-Hiyoshi-taisha_nishihongu2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hiyoshi-taisha_nishihongu2.jpg)

The typical shape of the back of a *Hiyoshi-zukuri* roof

4. **[^](#cite_ref-ko_4-0)** [History and Typology of Shrine Architecture](http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248), Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 2009
